title: When Symmetries Twist: Anomaly Inflow on Monodromy Defects
abstract: Monodromy defects describe a dynamical termination of topological symmetry operators, and are sourced by a localized background magnetic flux. We study their properties in gapped SPT phases and, by inflow, in gapless theories with an anomalous symmetry. The background flux acts as a source for the anomaly, impacting the definition of the monodromy defect, which can only be defined as a domain wall between the symmetry generator and a topological order induced by the anomaly. The topological dressing has several consequences, such as the presence of protected chiral edge modes on the defect's worldvolume, and the adiabatic pumping of gapless degrees of freedom bound to the localized flux. We verify our predictions in several examples, focusing on monodromy defects for anomalous chiral symmetries, both in the continuum and on the lattice.
summary: This paper explores how bulk anomalies dictate the physics of monodromy defects, demonstrating that these defects host protected edge modes and undergo spectral pumping. It provides a unified framework for understanding these defects as domain walls in both gapped SPT phases and gapless anomalous theories.
main_problem: The paper investigates the properties of monodromy defects, which are codimension-2 defects that terminate topological symmetry operators, specifically focusing on how bulk 't Hooft anomalies impact their physics.
main_result: The author shows that in anomalous gapless theories, monodromy defects are well-defined as domain walls between a symmetry defect and a topological decoration, leading to protected chiral edge modes and adiabatic spectral pumping of gapless degrees of freedom.
method: The study utilizes the anomaly inflow mechanism, transgression/slant product in group cohomology, radial quantization, and the Symmetry TFT (SymTFT) framework.
model_or_system: The paper considers gapped Symmetry Protected Topological (SPT) phases and gapless theories with anomalous global symmetries, including (3+1)d Dirac fermions, (1+1)d compact bosons, and (2+1)d scalar QED.
key_observables: Protected chiral edge modes, spectral flow/pumping of gapless degrees of freedom, and topological invariants like the chiral central charge and Hall response.
important_parameters:
The twist parameter (magnetic flux) $
u$, the symmetry group $G$ and its centralizer $C_G(g)$, and the $	heta$ angle periodicity.
main_assumptions: The analysis of $	heta$ angle periodicity and gravitational corrections is specifically focused on closed $Spin^c$ manifolds.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the monodromy defect as a dynamical termination of a symmetry operator or as being sourced by magnetic flux; Figure 2 shows the defect as a domain wall between a symmetry defect and a topological decoration.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining monodromy defects, moves to using topological defects as probes of SPTs, extends these concepts to anomalous gapless theories, and concludes with specific applications to free Dirac fermions and lattice models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A monodromy defect M(g) can be thought of as a dynamical termination of a symmetry operator U(g), which imposes twisted boundary conditions in the angular direction. Alternatively, it is sourced by a localized G-flux (fractional, in the discrete case).

Figure 2: Left: The monodromy defect M(g) defined as a domain wall between the symmetry defect U(g) and the topological decoration T(g). Right: The radial quantization frame S2 × R around a point on the monodromy defect. The surfaces U(g) and T(g) intersect the spatial S2 slice along meridians meeting at the poles.

Figure 3: a) An SPT in the presence of a topological defect bU(g), which is decorated by a lower di- mensional SPT τ(g). b) Terminating the invertible interface to define a topological monodromy defect induces a decoration by a lower-dimensional TQFT T(g). On the mapping torus Y d+1 = Xd × S1 the left and right sides are identified.

Figure 4: The inflow setup to describe an anomalous monodromy defect M(g) in a theory T . a) The boundary symmetry defect U(g) extends to the decorated SPT defect bU(g). b) Terminating the invertible interface to define a monodromy defect shows that M(g) is an interface between U(g) and T(g).

Figure 5: Using the topological decoration T(g) to correctly define a twisted Hilbert space. The cyan region represents the stacking of τ(g); rotating T(g) around the junction M(g) recasts the setup as a single composite defect U(g) ⊗T†(g).
